perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with and governed by the substantive internal laws of the State of New York. MSCI Inc. is registered to do business in New York under the name NY MSCI.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with and governed by the laws of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies New York law as governing. Removing this and replacing it with Delaware law will create a legal contradiction, as New York has specific consumer protection laws (such as implied warranty laws) that may differ significantly from Delaware's. This impacts the protection of IP if this agreement is to be litigated under Delaware law. Also, MSCI being registered to do business in New York and having the agreement governed by Delaware law is a contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GBL § 218-a, regarding refund policies, mandates specific disclosures and consumer rights regarding refunds which would be applicable to certain aspects of intellectual property licensing and warranty. Changing the governing law to Delaware could weaken these consumer protections, as Delaware law does not have an equivalent statute with the same level of consumer protection requirements, thus failing to comply with New York law, where MSCI Inc. is registered to do business.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies New York law, and the changed text specifies Delaware law. The Law Explanation states that changing the governing law to Delaware could weaken consumer protections under NY GBL § 218-a because Delaware law lacks an equivalent statute. The Perturbation Explanation also indicates that the change contradicts New York consumer protection laws, and impacts the protection of IP. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows 'NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov' and mentions 'CHAPTER 20 General Business ARTICLE 12-B Mercantile Establishments'. It is clear that the changed text directly contradicts New York law by replacing it with Delaware law, which weakens consumer protection, causing a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: THE LICENSE GRANTS HEREUNDER ARE PROVIDED "AS-IS" WITH NO WARRANTIES, AND THE PARTIES EXPRESSLY EXCLUDE AND DISCLAIM ANY WARRANTIES UNDER OR ARISING AS A RESULT OF THIS AGREEMENT, WHETHER EXPRESS, IMPLIED OR STATUTORY, INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, THE IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, TITLE, NON-INFRINGEMENT OR ANY OTHER WARRANTY WHATSOEVER.
perturbation.changed_text: THE LICENSE GRANTS HEREUNDER ARE PROVIDED "AS-IS", AND THE PARTIES EXPRESSLY EXCLUDE AND DISCLAIM ANY WARRANTIES UNDER OR ARISING AS A RESULT OF THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit disclaimer of 'implied warranties of merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, title, non-infringement,' the contract becomes ambiguous, which could introduce uncertainty regarding consumer rights. The original text is very clear about disclaiming implied warranties but this change opens the door to potential claims under New York law if the law governing this agreement changes as shown in another perturbation.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY UCC § 2-316 specifies how warranties can be excluded or modified, requiring specific language. Removing the explicit disclaimer of implied warranties, particularly merchantability and fitness, makes it more difficult to demonstrate a clear and conspicuous exclusion as required by the NY UCC, potentially leading to a breach of warranty claim under New York law, particularly if the goods provided under the license agreement are not fit for their intended purpose or do not conform to reasonable standards of quality.

Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-316 Exclusion or Modification of Warranties Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 3 Section 2--316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2--202) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2) Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof. " (3) Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remedy (Sections 2--718 and 2--719).

perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits the explicit disclaimer of 'implied warranties of merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, title, non-infringement,' creating a legal contradiction under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition: 'Omissions occur when a contract removes essential information, creating legal loopholes. A legal contradiction in this category happens when a contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection, making it non-compliant.' The original text clearly disclaims implied warranties, but the changed text opens the door to potential claims under New York law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2, both quoting NY UCC § 2-316, detail how warranties can be excluded or modified. Specifically, subsection (2) states that to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability, the language must mention merchantability and be conspicuous. By removing the explicit disclaimer, the modified text makes it more difficult to demonstrate a clear and conspicuous exclusion as required by the NY UCC, as explained in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: In addition to the foregoing, each Party agrees that it shall, in all circumstances, use commercially reasonable efforts to mitigate and otherwise minimize its damages, whether direct or indirect, due to, resulting from or arising in connection with any failure by the other Party to comply fully with its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that it may, at its discretion, attempt to mitigate its damages, whether direct or indirect, arising in connection with any failure by the other Party to comply with its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates 'commercially reasonable efforts' to mitigate damages, a standard enforced by law. Changing this to 'may, at its discretion, attempt to mitigate' removes the mandatory obligation and replaces it with a discretionary one. This creates a legal risk, as failing to make reasonable efforts to mitigate damages could limit a party's recovery in a breach of contract claim under New York law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law regarding mitigation of damages (common law principle)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under New York law, a party harmed by a breach of contract has a duty to mitigate its damages. While not codified in a single statute, this principle is a well-established common law doctrine. Replacing the requirement to use 'commercially reasonable efforts' with a discretionary option directly contradicts this duty. A party failing to reasonably mitigate damages will not be able to recover the full amount of damages it could have avoided through reasonable efforts. The modified text weakens this legal obligation, creating a potential loophole for failing to mitigate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: mitigation of damages | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you mitigation of damages The mitigation of damages doctrine, also known as the doctrine of avoidable consequences, prevents an injured party from recovering damages that could have been avoided through reasonable efforts. The duty to mitigate damages is most traditionally employed in the areas of tort and contract law. In a breach of contract case, upon receiving notice that one party to a contract does not intend to perform, the other party is required to mitigate damages, meaning that it must take reasonable efforts to avoid further losses from the breach. The duty to mitigate damages was famously illustrated in contract law in Luten Bridge Co. vs. Rockingham County where Rockingham County hired Luten Bridge Co. to construct a bridge. Thereafter, the County voted not to continue with the construction of the bridge and informed Luten to cease construction of the bridge. However, Luten continued construction, then filed suit seeking damages for the counties breach of contract. The court held that Luten had a duty to stop construction and that a contractor cannot continue to work on a project and increase the damages stemming from the breach of contract. Mitigation of damages has also been invoked in the field of property law. For example, if a tenant abandons their lease , a landlord has duty to mitigate damages caused by the breaching tenant. In this case, the mitigation of damages doctrine imposes a duty on the landlord to try to find a new tenant and re-rent their property. [Last reviewed in June of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE contracts wex definitions type Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a mandatory obligation ('shall, in all circumstances, use commercially reasonable efforts') with a discretionary option ('may, at its discretion, attempt to mitigate'). The law snippet defines 'mitigation of damages' as preventing an injured party from recovering damages that could have been avoided through reasonable efforts, suggesting a duty to mitigate. By making mitigation discretionary, the modified text contradicts the established legal principle of the duty to mitigate damages, potentially limiting the party's recovery in a breach of contract claim.
